title: Understanding the Significance of Joan of Arc's Trial in 1431

lesson.learn: Jean d'Arc, also known as Joan of Arc or the Maid of Orléans, was a French heroine who led the French army to several victories during the Hundred Years' War (1337-1453). Specifically, she won several battles in northeastern France, including the Siege of Orléans, which began in October 1428 and ended in May 1429. Unfortunately, her military success was short-lived, as she was captured by Burgundian forces in May 1430 and later sold to the English. Her trial began in Rouen, Normandy, in February 1431, lasting approximately 13 months, with five different judges hearing her case.
lesson.simpler: Imagine being a young woman who, at the age of 17, hears voices from God instructing her to drive the English out of France. This was the reality for Joan of Arc, who, despite limited education and experience, rose to prominence during the Hundred Years' War, ultimately leading the French army to victory at the Siege of Orléans in 1429. However, her subsequent capture by the English led to a series of interrogations and accusations of heresy, which would put her life at risk.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Joan of Arc's trial in 1431 serves as a prime example of how the English attempted to discredit her through manipulation, coercion, and false accusations, ultimately exemplifying the vulnerability of individuals in the medieval judicial system.
lesson.deeperDive: The trial of Joan of Arc was a complex affair, involving multiple interrogations, hearings, and court proceedings. On five separate occasions between February and May 1431, Joan, who claimed to have heard voices from Saint Catherine, Saint Margaret, and the Archangel Michael, was questioned by the English. The judges, attempting to prove her heresy, exploited inconsistencies in her testimony and presented coerced confessions, ultimately resulting in her conviction. Yet, what appeared to be an insurmountable defeat for Joan was later proved to be a turning point in French history, as her conviction sparked outrage among the French populace and galvanized support for Charles VII, King of France.
lesson.funFact: The ashes of Joan of Arc, collected by a sympathizer and buried in the Cathedral of Saint Catherine in Domremy in 1920, were later analyzed using radiocarbon dating in the 20th century, confirming their authenticity.
